case_name: Ashokbhai Shankar Chaudhary & vs Jayendra Virendra Sharma & on 22 July, 2014
court: High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad
facts: The plaintiff held an Agreement to Sale dated 07.03.2007 for 1/2 undivided share in immovable property (Revenue Survey No.544/3) and had been put into possession after part payment. Defendant No.1 later executed a Registered Sale Deed dated 28.10.2010 transferring his 1/2 share to defendant Nos.2 and 3 for Rs.32 lakhs. The plaintiff filed a suit for specific performance and sought injunctions to restrain transfer; the trial court granted interlocutory injunctions directing status quo of title and possession. Defendant Nos.2 and 3 appealed under Order 43 Rule 1(r), challenging the injunction as they claimed to be bona fide purchasers pendente lite.
arguments_claimant: The plaintiff argued the trial Judge rightly granted status quo injunction because defendant Nos.2 and 3 failed to show irreparable loss or damage and were not bona fide purchasers in light of prior public notice of the sale agreement. The plaintiff relied on the trial court's refusal to grant similar relief to the purchasers in their separate suit and cited Maharwal Khewaji Trust and earlier appellate orders confirming the injunction. The plaintiff contended that the sale agreement remained enforceable and specific performance could be sought against transferees who had notice.
arguments_respondent: Defendant Nos.2 and 3 contended they were bona fide purchasers for value under the Specific Relief Act and therefore protected from specific performance of a prior sale agreement. They argued the plaintiff failed to pay the balance consideration within the time fixed, the agreement was terminated by public notice, and multiplicity of proceedings/possibility of alienation justified allowing them to transfer rights. They relied on Gujarat Electricity Board and Thomson Press decisions to argue temporary injunction should not be granted merely on the possibility of multiplicity of proceedings or without considering their bona fide purchase status.
ratio_decidendi: The Appellate Court applied the settled rule that on interlocutory applications it is only required to see "whether the appellant-plaintiff has made out a prima facie case or not for grant of interim injunction." The court further held that "the Appellate Court may not interfere with the exercise of discretion of the court of first instance and substitute its own discretion except where the discretion has been shown to have been exercised arbitrarily, or capriciously or perversely or where the court had ignored the settled principles of law regulating grant or refusal of interlocutory injunctions." It reiterated the purpose of interlocutory injunctions as to "protect the plaintiff against injury by violation of his rights for which he could not adequately be compensated in damages" and the need to weigh that against the defendant's protection and the "balance of convenience." Applied to these facts, the court found defendant Nos.2 and 3 had notice of the earlier sale agreement (public notice) and thus could not be treated as bona fide purchasers under Section 19-B/Section 19 of the Specific Relief Act, so the trial Court's exercise of discretion to preserve status quo was not arbitrary or perverse.
final_order: The appeals are dismissed and the interim order of the trial Court directing status quo qua title and possession is affirmed; the connected civil applications do not survive and are dismissed.
summary: This is an appeal to the Gujarat High Court against a trial court's injunction preserving status quo over a disputed 1/2 undivided share in immovable property. The appellants (purchasers) claimed to be bona fide transferees pendente lite, while the plaintiff relied on an earlier Agreement to Sale and public notice. The High Court held the appellate court should not disturb the trial court's discretionary interlocutory order absent perversity and found the purchasers had notice and were not bona fide, so it dismissed the appeal and maintained the injunction. Practical effect: interlocutory protection for the plaintiff preserved pending trial, and purchasers cannot transfer assured rights where they had notice of prior agreement.
embedding_summary: Ashokbhai Shankar Chaudhary v. Jayendra Virendra Sharma, High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad, C/AO/196/2014, dated 22/07/2014. Dispute over immovable property (Revenue Survey No.544/3) arising from an Agreement to Sale dated 07.03.2007 (plaintiff paid part consideration Rs.1,50,000 and further Rs.50,000) and a subsequent Registered Sale Deed dated 28.10.2010 where defendant No.1 sold his 1/2 share to defendant Nos.2 and 3 for Rs.32,00,000. Key legal issues: interlocutory injunction under Order 39 Rules 1&2; scope of appellate interference under Order 43 Rule 1(r); bona fide purchaser doctrine and Section 19 / Section 19-B Specific Relief Act; effect of public notice and application of Sections 52 & 54 Transfer of Property Act; precedents relied upon include Maharwal Khewaji Trust (AIR 2005 SC 104), Ramdas v. Sitabai (AIR 2009 SC 2735), Thomson Press (2013)5 SCC 397, Wonder Ltd. v. Antox (1990 Supp SCC 727), Matrix Telecom (2011(3) GLR 1951) and Gujarat Electricity Board (AIR 1982 Gujarat 289). Outcome: appeal dismissed, trial court injunction (status quo of title and possession) affirmed, civil applications dismissed; no monetary compensation awarded.
legal_principles: Appellate court will not interfere with trial court's discretionary interlocutory orders unless the discretion was exercised arbitrarily, capriciously or perversely (appeal on principle).

Interlocutory injunctions exist to 'protect the plaintiff against injury by violation of his rights for which he could not adequately be compensated in damages' and require a prima facie case, balance of convenience and irreparable injury.

A transferee who acquires property 'in the teeth of an earlier Agreement to Sale' is not a bona fide purchaser protected from specific performance if the transferee had notice of the prior agreement (Section 19 / 19-B Specific Relief Act).
Co-sharers cannot be put into exclusive possession of a specific part of undivided property until partition/allocation is made by consent or decree (possession rights accrue after partition).

Time stipulated in contracts for immovable property is generally not treated as the essence of the contract; failure to pay by the stipulated date is a matter for trial and not necessarily a ground to terminate the contract at interlocutory stage.
sections_cited: Order 43 Rule 1(r) of the Code of Civil Procedure
Order 39 Rules 1 and 2 of the Code of Civil Procedure
Section 19 of the Specific Relief Act
Section 19-B of the Specific Relief Act
Sections 52 and 54 of the Transfer of Property Act
cases_cited: Matrix Telecom Pvt. Ltd. v. Matrix Cellular Services Pvt. Ltd., 2011(3) GLR 1951
Wonder Ltd. & Anr. v. Antox India Pvt. Ltd., 1990 (Supp.) SCC 727
Thomson Press (India) Ltd. v. Nanak Builders & Investors Pvt. Ltd., (2013) 5 SCC 397
Maharwal Khewaji Trust (Regd.), Faridkot v. Baldev Dass, AIR 2005 SC 104
Ramdas v. Sitabai & Ors., AIR 2009 SC 2735
Gujarat Electricity Board v. M/s. Maheshkumar & Co., AIR 1982 Gujarat 289
